records.summary: Human study evaluating a topical sea buckthorn oil emulsion in relation to skin hydration and transepidermal water loss over time.
records.abstractSnippet: Human study evaluating a topical sea buckthorn oil emulsion in relation to skin hydration and transepidermal water loss over time.
keyFindings: Skin hydration and transepidermal water loss were measured across an 84-day application period.
The active emulsion was compared with a base formulation in the same study design.
records.relevanceToCeovia: This study may help support ingredient-level discussion around skin hydration and barrier-related parameters, but it should not be used as CEOVIA product-efficacy proof.
records.citation: Khan BA, Akhtar N. Hippophae rhamnoides oil-in-water (O/W) emulsion improves barrier function in healthy human subjects. Pak J Pharm Sci. 2014;27(6):1919-1922.
records.safetyNotes: Topical emulsion study in healthy volunteers; route of administration and formulation differ from CEOVIA.
records.complianceNote: This study evaluates a specific topical formulation and does not represent CEOVIA or finished-product outcomes.
records.clinicalUseInsight: Relevant for discussing barrier-related hydration measures in a topical healthy-skin context, with clear formulation limits.
records.featuredNote: Human data on hydration and barrier-related parameters

records.summary: Review article discussing sea buckthorn bioactives in relation to skin and mucosal barrier function, inflammation-related pathways, and oxidative processes.
records.abstractSnippet: Review article discussing sea buckthorn bioactives in relation to skin and mucosal barrier function, inflammation-related pathways, and oxidative processes.
keyFindings: Reviews bioactive classes discussed in relation to barrier function, inflammation, and oxidative processes.
Synthesizes literature across skin and mucosal contexts rather than reporting a single intervention study.
records.relevanceToCeovia: This review may help contextualise ingredient-level discussion around barrier and mucosal biology, but it should not be used as finished-product efficacy evidence.
records.citation: Song X, Sun X, Yuan H, Tang Y, Zheng F. The role of sea buckthorn in skin and mucosal health: a review from an anti-inflammatory perspective. Front Pharmacol. 2025;16:1643146.
records.safetyNotes: Review-level source; no direct finished-product or CEOVIA-specific conclusions can be drawn from it.
limitations: Review-level evidence rather than a single controlled trial.
records.complianceNote: This is a review of ingredient-level evidence and does not establish product-specific clinical effects.
records.clinicalUseInsight: Relevant for literature-level context on barrier and inflammation-related pathways, without establishing intervention-specific outcomes.
records.featuredNote: Broad overview of mechanisms across skin and mucosal systems

records.summary: In vitro study in human dermal fibroblasts examining sea buckthorn pulp oil in relation to ultraviolet-induced cellular stress and collagen-associated measures.
records.abstractSnippet: In vitro study in human dermal fibroblasts examining sea buckthorn pulp oil in relation to ultraviolet-induced cellular stress and collagen-associated measures.
keyFindings: Assesses ultraviolet-related cell toxicity and collagen-associated measures in fibroblasts.
Reports pathway-level observations in an in vitro model.
records.relevanceToCeovia: This study may help support ingredient-level discussion around oxidative and photo-induced cellular stress pathways, but it should not be used as clinical or product-efficacy evidence.
records.citation: Okamoto T, Nakashima F, Shibata T, Mori D. Seabuckthorn (Hippophae rhamnoides, L.) pulp oil prevents ultraviolet-induced damage in human fibroblasts. Biosci Biotechnol Biochem. 2024;88(8):948-955.
records.safetyNotes: In vitro fibroblast study; no human clinical outcome or CEOVIA-specific formulation data are established.
records.complianceNote: This is a mechanistic laboratory study and does not establish clinical or finished-product outcomes.
records.clinicalUseInsight: Relevant for photo-induced oxidative-stress pathway context in fibroblast models, without direct clinical translation.

records.summary: Mechanistic macrophage study examining a sea buckthorn flavonoid fraction in relation to LPS-stimulated inflammatory signaling pathways.
records.abstractSnippet: Mechanistic macrophage study examining a sea buckthorn flavonoid fraction in relation to LPS-stimulated inflammatory signaling pathways.
keyFindings: The study focused on MAPK and NF-kB signaling activity in a macrophage model.
Observed findings were limited to laboratory inflammatory-response markers under in vitro conditions.
records.relevanceToCeovia: This study may help contextualise ingredient-level discussion around macrophage signaling pathways, but it should not be used as evidence of CEOVIA-specific immune effects.
records.citation: Yang B, et al. Flavonoids from sea buckthorn inhibit the lipopolysaccharide-induced inflammatory response in RAW264.7 macrophages through the MAPK and NF-kB pathways. Food Funct. 2017.
records.safetyNotes: Cell-model evidence only; no human immune safety or clinical outcome conclusions can be drawn from this record.
limitations: Pathway-level observations may not translate directly to whole-body immune outcomes.
records.complianceNote: This is a mechanistic laboratory study and does not establish clinical immune outcomes or finished-product effects.
records.clinicalUseInsight: Relevant for pathway-level context on cytokine-associated macrophage signaling, with no direct human immune inference.

records.summary: Randomized crossover human study evaluating sea buckthorn fruit puree in relation to plasma glucose measures in adults with impaired glucose regulation.
records.abstractSnippet: Randomized crossover human study evaluating sea buckthorn fruit puree in relation to plasma glucose measures in adults with impaired glucose regulation.
keyFindings: The study used a two-stage randomized crossover design with intervention and washout periods.
Observed outcomes were limited to glycemic measures collected within the studied food-puree intervention context.
records.relevanceToCeovia: This study may support cautious ingredient-level discussion around glycemic-response context, but it should not be used as CEOVIA product-efficacy proof.
records.citation: Effect of Sea Buckthorn on Plasma Glucose in Individuals with Impaired Glucose Regulation: A Two-Stage Randomized Crossover Intervention Study. Foods. 2021.
records.safetyNotes: Human food-intervention study in a specific impaired-glucose-regulation cohort; route and composition differ from CEOVIA.
limitations: Condition-specific cohort rather than a general population sample.
Food-puree intervention differs from CEOVIA formulation.
Findings should remain limited to the measured glycemic context.
records.complianceNote: This human study does not establish CEOVIA-specific metabolic outcomes or finished-product efficacy.
records.clinicalUseInsight: Relevant for discussing measured glycemic-response context in a food-intervention study, with population and formulation limits.
